sourceEvidence: Test 3.zip, IMG_9103 (paper page 6, section 'Digital Logic:'): '29. Which of the following Boolean equations describes the action of Fig. 2.5.2?' with options printed as: A. X = (A • B) + (B • C) where a SINGLE overbar runs across the whole 'A • B' group inside the parentheses (A circled in red as the key); B. X = (A • B) • (B + C); C. X = (A • B) + (B • C) where the bars are SEPARATE, one over A and one over B; D. X = (A • B) + C with a single overbar across the whole 'A • B' group. Verified at 3x zoom (q029_opts.png): the bar in A and D is one continuous stroke spanning A, the dot and B; in C there are two short strokes, one per letter.
note: Encoding defect on our side only: the group overbar in A and D was stored as U+0305 combining macrons smeared over the spaces and the bullet, which collapses A and C into look-alikes when the combining marks are stripped. Stem is verbatim-correct; options re-encoded with explicit NOT(...) so the De Morgan trap survives any renderer. No answer key touched (paper circles A).

sourceEvidence: Test 3.zip, IMG_9110 (paper page 13): '70. What is the throughput of simple/pure ALOHA?  A. 18.8 %  B. 18 %  C. 36.8 %  D. 36 %'. B is circled in red. Verified at 3x zoom (q070_opts.png): option A unambiguously reads '18.8 %' - the digit after the decimal point is an 8, not a 4. The student's own red handwriting next to the stem reads '18.4' and to the right 'slotted ALOHA = 36.8', i.e. the examinee noticed the paper's number was off.
note: The 18.8 % is a typo in the PAPER, not an OCR mangling; per the fidelity rule it is kept verbatim. Our extraction matches the source exactly. Separate observation for whoever owns keys (NOT changed here): the paper's own circled key is B (18 %), which agrees with the shipped key.

sourceEvidence: Data.pdf p.19, left column, second screenshot: 'Question' / 'Unlike filters queries can be saved as in a database.' / '(A) Database  (B) Filters  (C) Objects  (D) Any of the above' / 'View Answer  Ans: C  Objects'. Rendered at 600 dpi and cropped (q012_stem.png): there is NO underscore, NO blank gap and NO comma after 'filters' anywhere in the printed line - the words 'saved as in a database' run together with ordinary word spacing.
note: The blank was lost UPSTREAM of us - the source PDF is a screenshot of a web quiz whose underscore blank never rendered. Our text is verbatim-faithful to Data.pdf, so there is nothing to restore; inserting '______' and a comma would be inventing punctuation the paper lacks. Item is still answerable and the paper prints its own key (Ans: C, Objects). If the project wants a readable stem, that is an editorial decision for a human, not a source restoration.

sourceEvidence: Data.pdf p.19, right column: 'Question' / 'External database is' / '(A) Database created using DBMS package  (B) Database created in MS-Word  (C) Database created in EXCEL  (D) All of the above' / 'View Answer  Ans: A  Database created using DBMS package'. Verified at 600 dpi (q014_stem.png): the stem is exactly the three words 'External database is', with no trailing colon, no blank and no lost clause.
note: Nothing was dropped in extraction - the source item really is this terse. The oddity ('external' is never used by any option) is the source's own, and the paper supplies the key A. Our stem and all four options match the paper character for character.

sourceEvidence: Data.pdf p.19, bottom: 'A data dictionary doesn't provide information about:' / '(A) Where data is located  (B) The size of the disk storage device  (C) It allows a many-to-many relationship  (D) It is conceptually simple' / 'Correct Answer' / 'Answer: Option B'.
note: The mismatched options C and D are printed in the SOURCE exactly as we shipped them - they were not spliced in by our extraction. Per the fidelity rule they stay verbatim. Not quarantined because the paper still yields exactly one defensible answer and prints its own key (Option B), which matches the shipped key. The canonical distractors the triage expected ('whether the data is used by another program' etc.) do NOT appear anywhere on the page; reconstructing them would be invention.
